Thanks for coming back for the final round, Lucia. I want to run a few hypotheticals. First one: if you discovered a major security breach in your first week, what would your first move be?
That is a great question. If I found a breach that early, I would not panic. I would first evaluate the scope, then I would prioritize the systems most at risk before I told anyone outside the team.
Good instinct. Here is a harder one. If you had to choose between shipping fast and shipping safely, which would you prioritize?
Honestly, if the trade-off were that sharp, I would choose safety. My approach would be to delay the launch by a week rather than expose users to a risk I could not measure.
Interesting. And what criteria would you use to make that call?
I would weigh up three things: the size of the exposure, the number of users affected, and whether we could reverse the damage. If all three were high, I would hold the release.
Last one. If you were building an AI safety team from scratch, where would you start?
I would leverage the people I trust first. If I had a strong core of three, I would build the framework around them, because in my experience, judgment travels through people, not documents.
That is a great question. If I were in that situation, I would first evaluate the trade-offs.
My approach would be to prioritize the biggest risks first.
If I had to choose, I would protect the users every time.
I would leverage the experience I have built over the years.
Let me walk you through how I would think about it.

Let me tell you a secret about senior interviews. Half of my questions are hypothetical. What would you do if you found a major breach on your first week? If you had to choose between speed and safety, which would you pick? And here is what most people miss. I am not testing what you know. I am testing how you think. When a candidate says, if I were in that position, I would first evaluate the trade-offs, I lean in, because that is the sound of judgment. The grammar itself sends a signal. If plus the past, would plus a verb. It tells me you can step into a scenario that does not exist yet and reason your way through it. That is exactly what a leader does every single day. So do not fear the what-would-you-do question. It is your best chance to show me how your mind works.

So they asked me the classic one. What would you do in your first ninety days if you were building an AI safety team from scratch? Here is roughly what I said. If I were starting from zero, I would not rush to hire. I would first evaluate the real risks and the criteria that matter most to the board. Then I would prioritize the two or three that could cause the most harm. My approach would be to build trust before I build process, because if people did not trust the framework, they would never follow it. I would leverage the relationships I have built over the years, and I would weigh up every trade-off out loud, so the team could see my judgment, not just my decision. It is not about having every answer on day one. It is about showing how you would think your way to one.

That is a great question. If I were in that situation, I would first evaluate the trade-offs. Then I would prioritize the risks that matter most. My approach would be to leverage the people I trust. And I would weigh up every option before I decided anything.
